Documentation

🚀Getting Started📦Installation🔄Transactions & Async✏️CRUD Operations🔍Querying🤖Source Generators📡Change Data Capture🌍Spatial Indexing🏗️Architecture🆔Custom ID Converters⚡Benchmarks🔌Schema-less API🔎BLQL📈TimeSeries🗝️Key-Value Store

⚡ Benchmarks

BLite performance benchmarks vs LiteDB 5.0.21 and SQLite+JSON (Microsoft.Data.Sqlite + System.Text.Json) using BenchmarkDotNet on .NET 10.

📊 Benchmark Environment
  • OS: Windows 11 (10.0.22631.6649 / 23H2)
  • CPU: Intel Core i7-13800H — 14 physical cores, 20 logical @ 2.50GHz
  • Runtime: .NET 10.0.4 (X64 RyuJIT x86-64-v3)
  • BenchmarkDotNet: v0.15.8
  • Last Run: March 12, 2026

Engines: BLite (source-generated mappers) · LiteDB 5.0.21 (Connection=direct) · SQLite Microsoft.Data.Sqlite 10.0.4 + Dapper 2.1.66 (JSON blobs)

Document Schema

All benchmarks use a realistic e-commerce CustomerOrder document (~1–2 KB each):

CustomerOrder
├── Id, OrderNumber, PlacedAt, Status, Currency
├── Subtotal, TaxAmount, Total
├── Customer (CustomerContact)
│   ├── FullName, Email, Phone
│   └── BillingAddress (PostalAddress) → Street, City, ZipCode, Country
├── Shipping (ShippingInfo)
│   ├── Carrier, TrackingNumber, EstimatedDelivery
│   └── Destination (PostalAddress)
├── Lines (List<OrderLine> — 5 items)
│   └── each: Sku, ProductName, Qty, UnitPrice, Subtotal, Tags[3]
├── Notes (List<OrderNote> — 2 items)
└── Tags (List<string>)

1000 documents. Status distribution: ~25% each across pending · confirmed · shipped · delivered.

Summary

4.7x
vs LiteDB Single Insert
31x
vs SQLite Single Insert
5.8x
vs LiteDB FindById
3.3x
vs LiteDB Scan

Insert Performance

Single Document Insert

EngineMeanAllocatedRatio
BLite115.6 μs134.43 KBbaseline
LiteDB541.5 μs56.09 KB4.72x slower
SQLite+JSON3,608.8 μs10.02 KB31.46x slower

Batch Insert (1000 Documents, 1 Transaction)

EngineMeanAllocatedGen0Ratio
BLite9,113 μs31,242 KB2000baseline
LiteDB16,117 μs33,491 KB20001.77x slower
SQLite+JSON16,196 μs6,294 KB01.78x slower
⚠️ Note on SQLite allocations

SQLite reports minimal managed allocations because work is delegated to its native C library. Unmanaged memory is not captured by BenchmarkDotNet. BLite allocations are fully measured (100% managed code).

📊 BLite batch allocation history

Batch-1000 allocated 64,160 KB before the WAL page buffer reuse optimisation (March 2026), which reduced it to 31,242 KB (-51%). Each page is now written in-place when it already exists in the WAL cache for the current transaction, eliminating the previous data.ToArray() per write.

Read Performance

Results from DefaultJob — standard BenchmarkDotNet multi-iteration run (most reliable).

FindById — Primary Key Lookup

EngineMeanAllocatedRatio
BLite3.005 μs6.46 KBbaseline
LiteDB17.327 μs45.65 KB5.77x slower
SQLite+JSON28.425 μs9.33 KB9.46x slower

Scan — Filter by Status ("shipped", ~250 / 1000 results)

EngineMeanAllocatedRatio
BLite2,115 μs5,090 KBbaseline
LiteDB7,001 μs17,295 KB3.31x slower
SQLite+JSON6,156 μs7,803 KB2.91x slower

Serialization (BSON vs JSON)

Standalone serialization — no I/O, measured on a complex nested object.

Single Object

OperationBSON (BLite)JSON (STJ)Speedup
Serialize1.42 μs3.43 μs2.4x
Deserialize3.34 μs7.01 μs2.1x
Alloc (Serialize)0 B ✅1,880 B100%

Bulk (10,000 Objects)

OperationBSON (BLite)JSON (STJ)Speedup
Serialize14.99 ms21.40 ms1.43x
Deserialize18.92 ms42.96 ms2.27x
Alloc (Serialize)0 B ✅19.19 MB100%

Why BLite Is Faster

  • ✅ C-BSON Format — Field name compression (2-byte IDs vs full string keys)
  • ✅ Zero-Copy I/O — Direct Span<byte> operations throughout the pipeline
  • ✅ Memory Pooling — ArrayPool for buffer reuse; WAL cache pages updated in-place on repeated writes
  • ✅ Stack Allocation — stackalloc for temporary buffers
  • ✅ Source Generators — Compile-time serialization, no reflection at runtime
  • ✅ Group Commit — Background writer batches concurrent commits into one fsync, amortising disk write cost
  • ✅ vs LiteDB — LiteDB uses reflection-based BSON and a B+ tree requiring page splits; BLite's generated mappers and append-only WAL eliminate per-document reflection overhead

Running Benchmarks

# Full BenchmarkDotNet run (all engines, all categories)
dotnet run -c Release --project tests/BLite.Benchmark

# Filter to a specific category
dotnet run -c Release --project tests/BLite.Benchmark -- --filter "*Insert*"
dotnet run -c Release --project tests/BLite.Benchmark -- --filter "*Read*"

# Results:
# BenchmarkDotNet.Artifacts/results/
📄 Full Report

Complete benchmark data with error margins, standard deviations, and GC metrics: BENCHMARKS.md
